title: Hyperarousal vs. Hypoarousal: Two Sides of the Same Wound
researcherTopic: radical acceptance, RAIN technique, Buddhist psychology
metaDescription: Explore the dual states of hyperarousal and hypoarousal in complex trauma. Learn why your nervous system swings between agitation and shutdown, and how to heal.
imageDescription: A luminous, abstract image of golden light flowing through intricate, delicate circuitry, suggesting the pathways of the nervous system. The light is warm and healing, glowing softly against a neutral, calm background, evoking a sense of gentle repair and re-wiring.
htmlContent:
The world comes in too fast, a blur of sharp edges and loud noises that ricochet through the nervous system, leaving a hum of static in their wake. Or it doesn’t come in at all, receding to a distant, muffled signal behind a thick wall of fog, a place where time seems to stretch and thin into meaninglessness. This is the strange duality of a nervous system shaped by complex trauma... a constant, exhausting negotiation between too much and not enough, a pendulum swinging between two poles of a wound that is at its core one and the same. We have been taught to think of trauma responses in monolithic terms, as a simple switch flicked to “danger.” But the reality for those who have endured prolonged, developmental trauma is far more textured, a field of deep valleys and jagged peaks.

The Architecture of Agitation: Life in Hyperarousal

Hyperarousal is the state of being perpetually braced for impact. It is the body’s alarm system stuck in the “on” position, flooding the system with cortisol and adrenaline as if the original danger were still unfolding in the present moment. That becomes not a cognitive choice; it is a physiological reality, a deep echo of a time when heightened vigilance was essential for survival.

The thinking mind may know that the threat is over, but the body’s older, more primal intelligence continues to scan the horizon, interpreting neutral stimuli... a car backfiring, a raised voice, a sudden change in plans... as imminent threats. Life in this state is a marathon of anxiety, a relentless hum of activation that can sign as irritability, panic, emotional reactivity, and an inability to rest or feel at ease in one’s own skin. It’s the feeling of being haunted by a ghost that lives in your own cells, a constant companion whispering of dangers that are no longer there.

In my years of working in this territory, I have sat with countless individuals who describe this experience as a kind of internal combustion, an engine that simply will not turn off. They feel driven, restless, and exhausted all at once, their attention fragmented and their capacity for connection strained by the constant, low-level sense of emergency. Sleep offers little respite, often riddled with anxious dreams or the inability to fall asleep at all.

Relationships become minefields, as the hypervigilant nervous system misinterprets subtle cues as signs of rejection or abandonment. It is a significant misunderstanding to label this as a character flaw or a simple case of anxiety. It is the logical, albeit painful, outcome of a nervous system that learned, correctly, that the world was not a safe place. The work is not to silence the alarm, but to understand what it is trying to protect and to slowly, patiently, demonstrate that the war it is fighting is over.

The Quiet Collapse: Navigating the Territory of Hypoarousal

If hyperarousal is the roar, hypoarousal is the deafening silence that follows. It turns out to be the other side of the pendulum’s swing, a state of shutdown, disconnection, and numbness that can be just as debilitating, if not more so. Where hyperarousal is a mobilization of energy for fight or flight, hypoarousal is its conservation ~ a biological strategy of feigning death or playing dead when active defense is not a viable option.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Working with hypoarousal requires a different approach than its hyperaroused counterpart. It is not about calming down but about gently and safely reawakening. It is a process of inviting sensation back into the body, of re-establishing contact with the physical self without overwhelming the system. This might involve simple practices like noticing the feeling of the feet on the floor, the texture of a piece of fabric, or the subtle shifts in temperature in a room.

Bear with me on this one.

The Pendulum of Protection: Swinging Between States

For many with a history of complex trauma, the experience is not one of being stuck in either hyperarousal or hypoarousal, but of oscillating between the two. One might spend a day in a state of frantic, anxious energy, unable to settle or focus, only to crash into a state of striking exhaustion and disconnection the next. This swing is not random; it is the nervous system’s attempt to manage its energy and perceived threats. The intense activation of hyperarousal is metabolically expensive, and the system cannot sustain it indefinitely.

A client once described this as living in two different worlds, neither of which felt like home. In one, she was constantly on edge, reactive, and overwhelmed; in the other, she was numb, disconnected, and barely there. The work between these two poles is often where the deepest suffering lies, the feeling of being out of control of one’s own internal state. This oscillation is a key dynamic to understand when working within one's window of tolerance, the optimal zone of arousal where we can function most effectively.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

The Relational Echo: How Dysregulation Shapes Our Connections

We retreat into a protective shell, leaving partners, friends, and family feeling shut out and confused. This dynamic often recreates the very relational ruptures that caused the original trauma. We might find ourselves in a push-pull pattern, desperately seeking connection and then just as desperately needing space, leaving others with a sense of emotional whiplash. Or we may find it nearly impossible to form secure attachments at all, as the deep, instinctual part of us believes that connection itself is the source of danger.

This isn’t a failing of love or intention; it is a physiological reality. The social engagement system, the branch of our nervous system responsible for safe and reciprocal connection, is taken offline by the more primitive survival states of fight, flight, and freeze. We cannot will ourselves into connection when our body is screaming “danger!” or “disappear!” It turns out to be a core struggle for many, and learning to work through it is part of rebuilding relationships after trauma.

Beyond Management: Learning the Grammar of the Nervous System

So much of the conventional approach to these states is rooted in the idea of management or control ~ of trying to fix the nervous system as if it were a broken machine. But what if these states are not problems to be solved but signals to be understood? What if the nervous system is not broken but is, in fact, communicating something vital about our history and our needs?

This shift in perspective is at the heart of a more compassionate and effective approach to healing. It moves us from a relationship of opposition to one of collaboration with our own physiology. It asks us to become students of our own inner world, rather than its warden. At that threshold, the work of teachers like **Tara Brach** becomes so relevant, with her emphasis on what she calls radical acceptance.

Think about it this way. Your brain developed in an environment where certain things were true. Maybe love came with conditions. Maybe safety was unpredictable. Maybe the people who were supposed to protect you were the same people your nervous system needed protection from. That creates a particular kind of wiring.

Sebern Fisher's work on neurofeedback shows how these patterns literally show up in brainwave activity. Hypervigilant brains produce excess high-beta waves. Dissociative brains show too much slow-wave activity in the wrong places. This isn't metaphor. This is measurable, observable, and - critically - changeable.

I remember the first time I saw my own brain map. Seeing the dysregulation in color-coded images on a screen. Something shifted in that moment. It wasn't shame. It was relief. Because if it's in the wiring, then it's not a character flaw. It's not weakness. It's not something I chose. And if it got wired this way through experience, then experience can rewire it. Slowly. Imperfectly. But really.

We begin to see the protective intelligence behind the painful symptoms. What emerges is the process of learning to read the subtle cues of our own nervous system, a practice often referred to as understanding neuroception. It is about noticing the first flicker of activation before it becomes a full-blown panic attack, or the first hint of disconnection before it leads to a complete shutdown.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link).

The Practice Beyond Understanding

Healing from the dual wounds of hyperarousal and hypoarousal is not a linear process of climbing out of a hole. It is more like a slow, patient process of re-inhabiting a territory that was once a battlefield. It is about building the capacity to be with what is, to tolerate the discomfort of activation without being consumed by it, and to move through the emptiness of shutdown without losing oneself in it. What we find here is the architecture of returning... a gradual reconstruction of the internal sense of safety that allows the nervous system to finally come to rest.

Not what you expected, right?

It is less about arriving at a final destination of “healed” and more about becoming a skilled and compassionate navigator of our own inner space. This work is at the root somatic. It cannot be accomplished through intellect alone. We cannot think our way into a regulated nervous system. The body must have new experiences, experiences that directly contradict the old lessons of danger and threat.

This might look like the co-regulation that happens with a trusted therapist or friend, where one nervous system helps to soothe and stabilize another. It might be the gentle, rhythmic movements of a trauma-informed yoga practice, which allow for the safe discharge of stored survival energy. Or it could be the simple act of feeling the warmth of the sun on the skin and allowing that sensation to be a primary reality, even for just a moment. These are not trivial things; they are the very language the nervous system understands.

They are the raw data of safety that, over time, can begin to overwrite the old programming of danger. Ultimately, the path is one of integration. It is not about eliminating hyperarousal or hypoarousal entirely, as these are natural and necessary parts of our biological repertoire. It is about restoring them to their proper function, so they arise only in response to genuine, present-moment threats and recede when the danger has passed.

For calming an overactive nervous system, you could also try Pure Encapsulations GABA (paid link).

It is about reclaiming the vast and vibrant territory that exists between these two poles... the space of presence, connection, and vitality that is our birthright. That becomes not a quick fix or a simple solution. It is a lifelong practice of listening, of tending, of returning again and again to the quiet wisdom of the body. It is the slow, courageous work of coming home to ourselves. What would it mean to stop treating your nervous system as an adversary and begin to see it as a wise, albeit wounded, messenger?

What might change if the goal was not to silence its signals, but to finally learn its language? Deb Dana's practical application of polyvagal theory is a valuable companion The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana (paid link).
